item.title: Cook
item.description: Tested recipes. Filter by cuisine, course, diet, time, and difficulty.

item.title: Explore
item.description: Three families gather every entry. Pick a family for a focused walk, or jump straight to a category.

item.title: Pantry
item.description: What ingredients are, how to choose and store them, what to swap. Plus the equipment that earns its drawer.

item.title: Skills
item.description: Methods that show up across many recipes. Cuisines built from a pantry. Curated reading paths.

item.title: Weeknight pasta
item.description: Pasta dishes that hit the table in under 30 minutes without sacrificing technique.

item.title: All-purpose flour
item.description: Wheat flour at 10–12% protein. The middle of the road — strong enough for bread in a pinch, soft enough for cake if you handle it gently.

item.title: Assam tea
item.description: Robust, malty black tea from northeast India. The default base for masala chai and what gives English Breakfast its backbone.

item.title: Black peppercorns
item.description: Dried unripe fruit of Piper nigrum. Heat from piperine; aroma from terpenes that vanish on grinding.

item.title: Butter (unsalted)
item.description: Cow's-milk fat with ~16% water and ~2% milk solids. The solids are the flavor lever in cooking.

item.title: Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋)
item.description: Aged glutinous-rice vinegar from Zhenjiang. Dark, malty, smoky, and only mildly acidic — closer in spirit to balsamic than to white vinegar.

item.title: Cinnamon stick
item.description: Dried inner bark of Cinnamomum trees. Two main types: hot, sharp Cassia (the supermarket default) and floral, gentler Ceylon ("true" cinnamon).

item.title: Dark soy sauce (老抽)
item.description: Aged longer than light soy and cut with molasses or caramel. Used in small amounts for color and depth, not for primary salting.

item.title: Eggs (large)
item.description: Roughly 50g per large egg: ~30g white, ~18g yolk, ~6g shell. The white sets at 62°C, the yolk at 70°C — that gap is half of what you cook with.

item.title: Garlic
item.description: Allium sativum. Pungency comes from allicin, which only forms when you cut or crush a clove — and which mellows quickly with heat.

item.title: Ginger (fresh)
item.description: Rhizome of Zingiber officinale. Sharp, peppery heat from gingerol when raw; mellower and sweeter (zingerone) when cooked.

item.title: Light soy sauce (生抽)
item.description: The everyday Chinese seasoning soy — saltier, thinner, and lighter in color than dark soy. This is what most stir-fries and dipping sauces actually want.

item.title: Pecorino romano
item.description: Hard sheep's-milk cheese from Lazio. Salty, sharp, lactic — the canonical cheese for Roman pasta.

item.title: Chinese sesame paste (芝麻酱)
item.description: Toasted sesame seeds ground to a paste. Darker, deeper, and more bitter than tahini — the toast is the whole point.

item.title: Sichuan peppercorns
item.description: The husks of Zanthoxylum berries. Not actually pepper — they create má, the buzzing tingling numbness that defines Sichuan cooking.

item.title: Star anise
item.description: The dried fruit of Illicium verum, eight-pointed and intensely licorice-aromatic. The soul of Chinese five-spice and the unmistakable note in Thai iced tea.

item.title: Vanilla bean paste
item.description: Vanilla extract suspended in a syrup with the scraped seeds of the pod. Same flavor as a whole bean, a fraction of the price, and you get the visible black flecks.

item.title: Whole milk
item.description: Cow's milk at ~3.25% fat. The fat carries flavor and the casein binds tannins, which is why it shows up in everything from chai to custard.

item.title: Brown butter cacio e pepe
item.description: Roman three-ingredient pasta, weaponized: brown butter replaces olive oil to layer Maillard nuttiness over the pecorino lactic-tang and bloomed peppercorn piperine.

item.title: Brown butter sugar cookies
item.description: Sugar cookies built on melted brown butter — chewy and dense in the center, with a crackled sugar shell on top.

item.title: Chai pumpkin cupcakes
item.description: Pumpkin cupcakes with the spice infused into the milk like a real chai, then frosted with brown butter cream cheese.

item.title: Chinese tea eggs
item.description: Soft-boiled eggs cracked all over and steeped in spiced soy and black tea. The cracks become a marble pattern in the white.

item.title: Cocoa-osmanthus dun nai
item.description: Steamed milk pudding infused with cocoa nibs and finished with osmanthus and oolong — bitter, floral, silken.

item.title: Double dark chocolate crinkle cookies
item.description: Fudgy crinkle cookies built on melted dark chocolate, bloomed cocoa, and a coconut-oil tweak that keeps them glossy and chewy.

item.title: Vanilla dun nai (steamed milk custard)
item.description: Cantonese steamed milk custard with vanilla bean — silken, barely set, and finished in a Dutch oven using residual heat to avoid honeycomb.

item.title: House blend chai
item.description: A small-batch masala chai built on a freshly ground whole-spice blend. Two cups, fifteen minutes, no mix.
